edge: The widely used “optimism in the face of uncertainty” principle reduces a stochastic bandit problem to the construction of a confidence sequence for the unknown reward function.
The performance of the resulting bandit algorithm depends on the size of the confidence sequence, with smaller confidence sets yielding better empirical performance and stronger regret guarantees.
These confidence sequences allow for efficient action selection via convex programming.
We prove that a linear bandit algorithm based on our confidence sequences is guaranteed to achieve competitive worst-case regret.
We show that our confidence sequences are tighter than competitors, both empirically and theoretically.
Finally, we demonstrate that our tighter confidence sequences give improved performance in several hyperparameter tuning tasks.
The linear bandit problem is associated with the corresponding action.

The linear bandit problem has attracted attention because it is expressive enough to be a faithful model of many real-world decision-making problems, such as news recommendation and dynamic pricing, while being simple enough for theoretical analysis, represented by θ.
A popular way to design algorithms for linear bandits is to follow the principle of optimism in the face of uncertainty.
The principle of optimism in the face of uncertainty guides the design of algorithms for linear bandits.
Algorithms for linear bandits choose actions based on the expected reward function.
One can then run an upper confidence bound (UCB) algorithm in the context of linear bandits.
The popularity of UCB algorithms stems from the fact that they come with worst-case regret guarantees.
UCB algorithms are popular due to their worst-case regret guarantees, which ensure a certain level of performance regardless of the scenario.
UCB algorithms often perform well in practice, contributing to their popularity.
The confidence bounds of CMM-UCB are compared to the true function to show that they are tighter than those of OFUL.
CMM-UCB and AMM-UCB confidence bounds are compared to demonstrate that CMM-UCB confidence bounds are slightly tighter.
The size of the confidence sets impacts the regret bound of the algorithm, influencing its performance.
Smaller subsets in the confidence sequence lead to better regret bound and overall algorithm performance in practice.
The tail bound for martingale mixtures is used to construct confidence sequences, providing a useful tool in the context.
In the linear bandit problem, computing the UCB involves solving a maximization problem with the UCB being a convex program.
UCB computation involves utilizing gradient-based methods to maximize the UCB over actions.
CMM-UCB employs a convex solver as part of its algorithm for maximizing the UCB.
Convex Martingale Mixture UCB (CMM-UCB) is a method employed for UCB maximisation and its gradients are calculated using a convex solver.

Analytic Martingale Mixture UCB (AMM-UCB) utilizes weak Lagrangian duality to obtain an analytic upper bound on the UCB, and the gradient can be computed in closed-form or via standard automatic differentiation procedures.
OFUL algorithm is compared to linear bandits used in state-of-the-art algorithms, showing that the UCBs in CMM-UCB and AMM-UCB are tighter.

Stochastic linear bandit problems can be seen as a restricted version of the stochastic kernelised bandit problem.

node_2: maximisation of ucb at the action a
edge: To run the LinUCB action selection rule with the confidence sequence, the goal is to maximize UCBΘt(a) concerning the action a.
UCB value at action 'a' is the solution that maximizes the UCBΘt(a), where UCBΘt(a) is a convex optimization problem that can be efficiently solved via convex programming

The action sets have finite cardinality and UCBΘt(a) can be maximized by solving for each 'a' in the action set and comparing the solutions. If the action sets are continuous subsets of RdA, exact maximization of UCBΘt(a) is generally infeasible

When the feature map ϕ is linear in 'a', UCBΘt(·) becomes the maximum over a set of linear functions, which is a convex function of 'a'. Exact maximization of UCBΘt(a) is generally NP-hard when the feature map ϕ is linear
